topic: Area B: Personal and Social Life
subject: spanish

items.q: What is the Spanish word for 'mobile phone' (in Spain)?
items.misconception: El celular (which is used in Latin America).
items.why: Vocabulary varies by region. Spain: móvil, ordenador (computer), coche (car). Latin America: celular, computadora, carro/auto. Students mix these. Examiners accept both, but consistency matters. Móvil is most common in GCSE/A-level.

items.q: Why does 'inglés' have a written accent?
items.correct: It stresses the final syllable while ending in -s, which is unusual.
items.misconception: All Spanish words have accent marks.
items.why: Students treat accents as random. They follow STRESS RULES: words stressed normally don't need accents; the accent shows when stress falls outside the default position (or distinguishes homophones).

items.q: Where do most descriptive adjectives go relative to the noun?
items.misconception: Before the noun, as in English.
items.random_wrong: Either position with the same meaning.
items.why: Students apply English order. In Spanish, descriptive adjectives typically FOLLOW the noun. Some adjectives precede (limiting adjectives like buen, gran) and can change meaning - gran amigo (great friend) vs amigo grande (big friend).
Correct answer: Me gusta el español.. “Me gusto el español.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which sentence means 'Maths is more difficult than English'?
items.correct: Las matemáticas son más difíciles que el inglés.
items.misconception: Las matemáticas es más difícil que el inglés.
items.random_wrong: Las matemáticas son menos difíciles que el inglés.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Las matemáticas son más difíciles que el inglés.. “Las matemáticas es más difícil que el inglés.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many subject pronouns does Spanish have (yo, tú, él/ella/usted, nosotros, vosotros, ellos/ellas/ustedes)?
items.why: Exact answer: 6 pronouns. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
